Name: Atoms, elements and compounds
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use names and symbols of the first 20 elements; name compounds from formulae; write word and balanced chemical equations.
Description: Everything is composed of atoms, the smallest unit of an element. Elements consist of one type of atom represented by chemical symbols, while compounds consist of two or more elements chemically joined in fixed proportions, representable by formulae.

Name: Mixtures
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe and explain physical processes of separation; suggest suitable techniques for mixtures when given specific information.
Description: A mixture contains two or more substances not chemically joined, retaining their individual chemical properties. They are separable via physical processes like filtration, crystallisation, distillation, and chromatography without chemical reactions.

Name: The development of the model of the atom
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe why evidence led to changes in atomic models; explain the difference between the plum pudding and nuclear models.
Description: The atomic model evolved from indivisible spheres to the plum pudding model (positive ball with embedded electrons), then to the nuclear model following the alpha particle scattering experiment, eventually including Bohr’s shells and Chadwick’s neutrons.

Name: Relative electrical charges of subatomic particles
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use the nuclear model to describe atoms; relate the number of protons to the atomic number.
Description: Atoms consist of protons (+1), neutrons (0), and electrons (-1). Because the number of protons equals the number of electrons, atoms are electrically neutral. The atomic number is defined by the unique number of protons in an element.

Name: Size and mass of atoms
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: calculate numbers of protons, neutrons, and electrons given atomic/mass numbers; relate the scale of atoms to the physical world.
Description: Atoms are extremely small (radius ~0.1 nm), with a central nucleus 1/10,000th the size containing almost all the mass. Mass number is the sum of protons and neutrons; isotopes are atoms of the same element with different neutron counts.

Name: Relative atomic mass
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: calculate the relative atomic mass of an element given the percentage abundance of its isotopes.
Description: The relative atomic mass (Ar) is an average value that accounts for the mass and the abundance of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element.

Name: Electronic structure
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: represent the electronic structures of the first twenty elements of the periodic table in both numerical and diagrammatic forms.
Description: Electrons occupy specific energy levels (shells) in a fixed order. The lowest energy levels closest to the nucleus are filled first. Sodium, for example, is represented as 2, 8, 1.

Name: The periodic table
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: explain how an element's position relates to its electronic arrangement; predict reactions and reactivity based on table position.
Description: Elements are organized by atomic number into groups (columns) and periods (rows). Elements in the same group share the same number of outer-shell electrons, resulting in similar chemical properties.

Name: Development of the periodic table
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe the steps in the development of the periodic table; explain how Mendeleev’s predictions were validated.
Description: Early tables were based on atomic weights and were often incomplete. Mendeleev improved the table by leaving gaps for undiscovered elements and switching order where properties dictated, with later knowledge of isotopes explaining the weight discrepancies.

Name: Metals and non-metals
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: explain differences between metals and non-metals based on physical/chemical properties; relate atomic structure to position in the table.
Description: Metals are found on the left and bottom of the table and react to form positive ions. Non-metals are on the right and top, forming negative ions or covalent bonds. These differences are rooted in the arrangement of their electrons.

Name: Group 0
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: explain how properties depend on the outer shell; predict properties from trends down the group.
Description: Known as Noble Gases, these are unreactive non-metals with stable electron arrangements (8 outer electrons, except Helium which has 2). Their boiling points increase as you move down the group.

Name: Group 1
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe reactions with oxygen, chlorine, and water; explain how properties depend on the outer shell; predict trends.
Description: Known as Alkali Metals, they have one outer electron, making them highly reactive. Reactivity increases down the group as the outer electron is further from the nucleus and more easily lost.

Name: Group 7
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe reactions with metals and non-metals; explain properties based on the outer shell; predict trends down the group.
Description: Known as Halogens, these non-metals consist of diatomic molecules. Reactivity decreases down the group, while relative molecular mass, melting point, and boiling point all increase.

Name: Comparison with Group 1 elements
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe the difference between transition metals and Group 1 in terms of melting points, densities, strength, hardness, and reactivity.
Description: Transition elements are metallic with higher melting points and densities than Group 1. They are generally harder, stronger, and much less reactive with water and oxygen.

Name: Typical properties
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: exemplify general properties (coloured compounds, multiple ions, catalysis) by reference to Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu.
Description: Transition metals are characterized by their ability to form ions with different charges, produce vibrant coloured compounds, and act as effective catalysts in many chemical reactions.

Name: Chemical bonds
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain chemical bonding in terms of electrostatic forces and the transfer or sharing of electrons.
Description: There are three primary types of strong chemical bonds: ionic, covalent, and metallic. Ionic bonding involves electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions (metal to non-metal). Covalent bonding involve atoms sharing pairs of electrons (non-metals). Metallic bonding involves atoms sharing a "sea" of delocalised electrons (metals and alloys).

Name: Ionic bonding
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: draw dot and cross diagrams for ionic compounds formed by metals in Groups 1 and 2 with non-metals in Groups 6 and 7; work out the charge on ions from the group number of the element.
Description: During ionic bond formation, electrons are transferred from metal atoms (forming positive ions) to non-metal atoms (forming negative ions). The resulting ions achieve the stable electronic structure of a noble gas. These diagrams visualize the transfer of outer-shell electrons.

Name: Ionic compounds
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: deduce that a compound is ionic from a diagram of its structure; describe the limitations of using different models (ball and stick, etc.); work out the empirical formula from a model.
Description: Ionic compounds form giant ionic lattices where strong electrostatic forces of attraction act in all directions between oppositely charged ions. While models like ball-and-stick show arrangements, they have limitations in representing the continuous nature and scale of the lattice.

Name: Covalent bonding
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: draw dot and cross diagrams for molecules (H2, Cl2, O2, N2, HCl, H2O, NH3, CH4); represent bonds with lines; describe model limitations; deduce molecular formulas from diagrams.
Description: Covalent bonds are formed when atoms share pairs of electrons to achieve stability. These bonds are very strong and can result in small molecules, large molecules like polymers, or giant covalent structures like diamond and silica.

Name: Metallic bonding
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to recognise substances as metallic giant structures from diagrams showing their bonding.
Description: Metals consist of giant structures where atoms are arranged in a regular pattern. Electrons in the outer shell are delocalised, meaning they are free to move throughout the entire structure, creating strong metallic bonds through shared delocalised electrons.

Name: The three states of matter
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: predict states at different temperatures from data; explain change of state temperatures in terms of energy and bonding; recognise bulk properties; (HT only) explain particle theory limitations.
Description: Matter exists as solid, liquid, or gas. The temperature of melting or boiling depends on the energy required to overcome forces between particles. This energy requirement is determined by the type of bonding and the structure of the substance.

Name: State symbols
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to include appropriate state symbols in chemical equations for the reactions in this specification.
Description: Chemical equations use specific notation to indicate the physical state of reactants and products: (s) for solid, (l) for liquid, (g) for gas, and (aq) for aqueous solutions (dissolved in water).

Name: Properties of ionic compounds
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain the high melting points and electrical conductivity of ionic compounds in terms of their giant lattice and mobile ions.
Description: Giant ionic lattices require significant energy to break the strong electrostatic forces, leading to high melting and boiling points. They conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved because the ions are free to move and carry charge.

Name: Properties of small molecules
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to use the idea that intermolecular forces are weak compared with covalent bonds to explain the bulk properties of molecular substances.
Description: Small molecules have strong internal covalent bonds but very weak intermolecular forces. Since only these weak forces are overcome during melting or boiling, these substances have low melting/boiling points and do not conduct electricity as they lack overall charge.

Name: Polymers
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to recognise polymers from diagrams showing their bonding and structure.
Description: Polymers are comprised of very large molecules. Atoms within the chain are linked by strong covalent bonds, while the intermolecular forces between the large chains are strong enough to make polymers solid at room temperature.

Name: Giant covalent structures
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to recognise giant covalent structures from diagrams showing their bonding and structure.
Description: These are solid substances with exceptionally high melting points. Every atom is linked to others by strong covalent bonds that must be broken to melt the substance. Key examples include diamond, graphite, and silicon dioxide.

Name: Properties of metals and alloys
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain why alloys are harder than pure metals in terms of distortion of the layers of atoms in the structure of a pure metal.
Description: Pure metals have atoms arranged in regular layers that can slide over each other, making them soft. Alloys contain different sized atoms that distort these layers, making it harder for them to slide and thus making the material stronger.

Name: Metals as conductors
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain the electrical and thermal conductivity of metals in terms of delocalised electrons.
Description: Metals are excellent conductors of both electricity and thermal energy. This is because delocalised electrons are free to move throughout the giant metallic structure, allowing them to carry electrical charge and transfer energy efficiently.

Name: Diamond
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain the properties of diamond in terms of its structure and bonding.
Description: In diamond, each carbon atom is covalently bonded to four other carbon atoms. This giant covalent structure is extremely hard, has an incredibly high melting point, and cannot conduct electricity because there are no free electrons.

Name: Graphite
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain the properties of graphite in terms of its structure and bonding; recognise that graphite has delocalised electrons.
Description: Graphite is composed of layers of hexagonal rings where each carbon atom forms three bonds. There are no covalent bonds between layers, allowing them to slide. One electron per atom is delocalised, allowing graphite to conduct electricity and heat.

Name: Graphene and fullerenes
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recognise graphene and fullerenes from diagrams; give examples of uses of fullerenes, including carbon nanotubes.
Description: Graphene is a single, two-dimensional layer of graphite. Fullerenes are carbon molecules with hollow shapes (like C60). Carbon nanotubes are cylindrical fullerenes with high tensile strength and high electrical conductivity, useful in nanotechnology.

Name: Sizes of particles and their properties
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to compare ‘nano’ dimensions to typical dimensions of atoms and molecules.
Description: Nanoscience involves particles 1–100 nm in size. As particles get smaller, their surface area to volume ratio increases dramatically. This gives nanoparticles unique properties compared to the same material in bulk form.

Name: Uses of nanoparticles
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: evaluate the use of nanoparticles for a specified purpose given information; explain possible risks associated with nanoparticles.
Description: Nanoparticles have high-tech applications in medicine, electronics, cosmetics (sun creams), and catalysts. However, their small size means they may pose unique health and environmental risks that require careful research and peer review.

Name: Conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: understand the use of multipliers in equations (coefficients) and subscripts within a formula; explain why mass is conserved in a balanced symbol equation.
Description: The law of conservation of mass states that no atoms are lost or made during a chemical reaction, ensuring the mass of products equals the mass of reactants. This fundamental principle requires chemical reactions to be represented by balanced symbol equations.

Name: Relative formula mass
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the percentage by mass in a compound given the relative formula mass (Mr) and relative atomic masses (Ar).
Description: The relative formula mass (Mr) of a compound is the sum of the relative atomic masses of every atom shown in the formula. In balanced equations, the sum of Mr of reactants equals the sum of Mr of products.

Name: Mass changes when a reactant or product is a gas
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain observed changes in mass in non-enclosed systems using the balanced symbol equation and the particle model.
Description: Apparent changes in mass during reactions usually occur in non-enclosed systems where a gaseous reactant is taken in (increasing mass) or a gaseous product escapes into the atmosphere (decreasing mass), such as in thermal decomposition.

Name: Chemical measurements
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: represent the distribution of results and make estimations of uncertainty; use the range of measurements about the mean as a measure of uncertainty.
Description: Scientific measurements always involve a degree of uncertainty. This uncertainty can be quantified and estimated by analyzing the spread (range) of experimental data points relative to their calculated mean.

Name: Moles (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: use the relative formula mass of a substance to calculate the number of moles in a given mass and vice versa; understand the Avogadro constant.
Description: A mole is the unit for chemical amount (mol). One mole of any substance contains $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ particles (Avogadro constant). The mass of one mole of a substance in grams is numerically equal to its relative formula mass.

Name: Amounts of substances in equations (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the masses of reactants and products from a balanced symbol equation and the mass of a given reactant or product.
Description: Balanced symbol equations can be interpreted in terms of moles, allowing for the calculation of the exact masses of substances involved in a reaction based on their stoichiometric ratios.

Name: Using moles to balance equations (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: balance an equation given the masses of reactants and products; change the subject of a mathematical equation.
Description: The balancing numbers (coefficients) in a symbol equation can be derived experimentally by converting measured masses of reactants and products into molar amounts and finding their simplest whole-number ratios.

Name: Limiting reactants (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain the effect of a limiting quantity of a reactant on the amount of products it is possible to obtain in terms of moles or masses.
Description: In many reactions, one reactant is used in excess to ensure completion. The reactant that is completely consumed is the limiting reactant, as it determines the maximum possible amount of product that can be formed.

Name: Concentration of solutions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the mass of solute in a given volume of known concentration ($g/dm^3$); (HT only) explain how mass and volume relate to concentration.
Description: Solution concentration is often measured in mass per volume ($g/dm^3$). The amount of solute present depends on both the concentration of the solution and the total volume of the liquid used.

Name: Percentage yield
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the percentage yield of a product from the actual yield; (HT only) calculate theoretical mass from a given mass of reactant and balanced equation.
Description: Percentage yield compares the actual mass of product obtained to the maximum theoretical mass. Yield is rarely 100% due to reversible reactions, loss during separation, or side reactions.

Name: Atom economy
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the atom economy of a reaction from the balanced equation; (HT only) explain why a specific reaction pathway is chosen for sustainable development.
Description: Atom economy measures the proportion of starting materials that end up as useful products. High atom economy is vital for sustainable development and economic efficiency as it minimizes waste.

Name: Using concentrations of solutions in mol/dm3 (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain how concentration ($mol/dm^3$) relates to the mass of solute and volume; calculate concentration from titration data.
Description: Concentration can be expressed in $mol/dm^3$. If the volume of two reacting solutions is known alongside the concentration of one, the concentration of the other can be determined through titration calculations.

Name: Use of amount of substance in relation to volumes of gases (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the volume of a gas at room temperature and pressure from its mass and Mr; calculate volumes of gaseous reactants and products from balanced equations.
Description: Equal moles of all gases occupy the same volume under identical conditions. At room temperature and pressure ($20^\circ\text{C}$ and 1 atm), one mole of any gas has a volume of $24\text{ dm}^3$.

Name: Metal oxides
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to explain reduction and oxidation in terms of loss or gain of oxygen.
Description: Metals undergo oxidation reactions when they react with oxygen to produce metal oxides. In this context, oxidation is defined specifically as the gain of oxygen, while reduction is the loss of oxygen.

Name: The reactivity series
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recall and describe reactions of K, Na, Li, Ca, Mg, Zn, Fe, and Cu with water or acids; explain reactivity in terms of ion formation; deduce an order of reactivity from experimental results.
Description: The reactivity of a metal is determined by its tendency to lose electrons and form positive ions. Metals are arranged in a reactivity series; more reactive metals can displace less reactive metals from their compounds in aqueous solutions.

Name: Extraction of metals and reduction
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: interpret or evaluate metal extraction processes; identify substances oxidised or reduced in terms of oxygen transfer.
Description: Unreactive metals like gold are found in their native state, but most metals exist as compounds. Metals less reactive than carbon are extracted by reduction (loss of oxygen) using carbon as a reducing agent.

Name: Oxidation and reduction in terms of electrons (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: write ionic equations for displacement reactions; identify species oxidised and reduced in symbol or half equations.
Description: At the atomic level, oxidation is defined as the loss of electrons (OIL), and reduction is the gain of electrons (RIG). These simultaneous processes in a reaction are known as redox reactions.

Name: Reactions of acids with metals
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: (HT only) explain redox reactions in terms of electron transfer; identify species oxidised and reduced in equations.
Description: Acids react with metals (specifically Mg, Zn, and Fe) to produce a salt and hydrogen gas. These are redox reactions where the metal atoms lose electrons and hydrogen ions gain them.

Name: Neutralisation of acids and salt production
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: predict products from given reactants; use formulae of common ions to deduce salt formulae.
Description: Acids are neutralised by alkalis, bases, and metal carbonates to produce salts and water. Carbonates also produce carbon dioxide. The specific salt depends on the acid used (e.g., hydrochloric acid produces chlorides).

Name: Soluble salts
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe how to make pure, dry samples of named soluble salts from an insoluble oxide or carbonate.
Description: Soluble salts are prepared by reacting an acid with an excess of an insoluble base. The mixture is filtered to remove unreacted base and the resulting solution is crystallised to obtain the solid salt.

Name: The pH scale and neutralisation
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe the use of indicators or probes to measure pH; use the pH scale to identify acidic, alkaline, or neutral solutions.
Description: The pH scale (0-14) measures acidity (H+ ions) and alkalinity (OH- ions). Neutralisation occurs when H+ ions from an acid react with OH- ions from an alkali to produce water ($H^+ + OH^- \rightarrow H_2O$).

Name: Titrations (chemistry only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe how to carry out titrations to find reacting volumes accurately; (HT only) calculate chemical quantities in $mol/dm^3$ and $g/dm^3$.
Description: Titration is an analytical technique used to determine the exact volume of an acid and alkali that react completely. It requires the use of a burette, pipette, and a suitable indicator to identify the end-point.

Name: Strong and weak acids (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: use and explain the terms dilute, concentrated, weak, and strong; describe neutrality and acidity in terms of H+ concentration and pH.
Description: Strong acids (e.g., HCl) fully ionise in aqueous solution, while weak acids (e.g., ethanoic) only partially ionise. A decrease of one pH unit represents a tenfold increase in hydrogen ion concentration.

Name: The process of electrolysis
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: (HT only) write, complete, and balance half equations for electrode reactions.
Description: Electrolysis is the decomposition of an electrolyte (a molten or dissolved ionic compound) using an electric current. Positive ions move to the cathode (negative electrode) and negative ions move to the anode (positive electrode).

Name: Electrolysis of molten ionic compounds
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to predict the products of the electrolysis of binary ionic compounds in the molten state.
Description: When a molten binary ionic compound is electrolysed, the metal is produced at the cathode as ions are reduced, and the non-metal is produced at the anode as ions are oxidised.

Name: Using electrolysis to extract metals
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain why a mixture (cryolite) is used as the electrolyte; explain why the carbon anode must be replaced.
Description: Electrolysis extracts metals too reactive to be reduced by carbon. In aluminium extraction, aluminium oxide is dissolved in molten cryolite to lower the melting point, saving energy; the oxygen produced reacts with the carbon anodes, requiring their regular replacement.

Name: Electrolysis of aqueous solutions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to predict the products of the electrolysis of aqueous solutions containing a single ionic compound.
Description: In aqueous electrolysis, hydrogen is produced at the cathode if the metal is more reactive than hydrogen. At the anode, oxygen is produced unless halide ions are present, in which case the halogen is formed.

Name: Representation of reactions as half equations (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to represent cathode and anode reactions as half equations involving the loss or gain of electrons.
Description: Half equations show the gain of electrons (reduction) at the cathode and the loss of electrons (oxidation) at the anode. They provide a detailed view of the electron transfer occurring at each electrode.

Name: Energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: distinguish between exothermic and endothermic reactions on the basis of temperature change; evaluate uses and applications of energy transfer reactions given appropriate information.
Description: Energy is conserved in chemical reactions; the total energy in the universe remains constant. Exothermic reactions transfer energy to the surroundings, resulting in a temperature increase (e.g., combustion, neutralisation). Endothermic reactions take in energy from the surroundings, causing a temperature decrease (e.g., thermal decomposition).

Name: Reaction profiles
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: draw simple reaction profiles for exothermic and endothermic reactions; identify reactions as exo or endo from profiles; explain that activation energy is the energy needed for a reaction to occur.
Description: Reaction profiles (energy level diagrams) visualize the relative energies of reactants and products. They illustrate the overall energy change and the activation energy—the minimum energy particles must possess to react upon collision. A curved line represents the energy path as the reaction proceeds.

Name: The energy change of reactions (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to calculate the energy transferred in chemical reactions using bond energies supplied.
Description: During reactions, energy is absorbed to break bonds in reactants (endothermic process) and released when new bonds form in products (exothermic process). The overall energy change is the sum of energy needed to break bonds minus the energy released when bonds are formed.

Name: Cells and batteries
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to interpret data for relative reactivity of different metals and evaluate the use of cells.
Description: Cells produce electricity through chemical reactions between electrodes and an electrolyte. Voltage depends on the metals used and the electrolyte. Batteries are multiple cells in series. Non-rechargeable cells stop when reactants are exhausted, while rechargeable cells reverse the reaction using an external current.

Name: Fuel cells
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: evaluate the use of hydrogen fuel cells in comparison with rechargeable cells/batteries; (HT only) write the half equations for the electrode reactions in the hydrogen fuel cell.
Description: Fuel cells use an external fuel source (like hydrogen) and oxygen to produce a potential difference through electrochemical oxidation. In a hydrogen fuel cell, the only waste product is water, making them a clean alternative to traditional batteries, though they involve different technical and economic trade-offs.

Name: Calculating rates of reactions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: calculate the mean rate of a reaction from given information; draw and interpret graphs showing quantity over time; draw tangents to curves to measure rate; (HT only) calculate the gradient of a tangent as a measure of rate at a specific time.
Description: Reaction rates are determined by monitoring the depletion of reactants or the accumulation of products over time. Measurements are typically taken in grams ($g$) or volume ($cm^3$), resulting in units like $g/s$ or $cm^3/s$. Higher Tier students also express rates in $mol/s$ using molar quantities.

Name: Factors which affect the rates of chemical reactions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to recall how changing factors (concentration, pressure, surface area, temperature, and catalysts) affects the rate of chemical reactions.
Description: Several physical variables can be manipulated to control reaction speed. These include increasing the concentration of solutions, raising the pressure of reacting gases, increasing the surface area of solid reactants, raising the temperature, or introducing a catalyst to the system.

Name: Collision theory and activation energy
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: predict and explain the effects of changing conditions using collision theory; explain the effects of surface area to volume ratio; use ideas about proportionality to explain factor effects.
Description: According to collision theory, reactions occur only when particles collide with energy equal to or greater than the activation energy. Rate increases are caused by increasing the frequency of collisions (via concentration, pressure, or surface area) or by increasing the energy and frequency of collisions (via temperature).

Name: Catalysts
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: identify catalysts in reactions from their effect and absence in the chemical equation; explain catalytic action in terms of activation energy.
Description: Catalysts are substances that speed up chemical reactions without being consumed. They function by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. Biological catalysts are known as enzymes. This effect is often visualized using reaction profiles.

Name: Reversible reactions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to understand that the direction of reversible reactions can be changed by changing the conditions.
Description: In a reversible reaction, the products can react to reform the original reactants. This is denoted by the $\rightleftharpoons$ symbol. The final direction of the reaction is determined by the environmental conditions imposed on the system.

Name: Energy changes and reversible reactions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to understand that if a reversible reaction is exothermic in one direction, it is endothermic in the opposite direction, with equal energy transfer.
Description: Reversible reactions involve reciprocal energy changes. If the forward reaction releases a specific amount of heat (exothermic), the reverse reaction must absorb the exact same amount of heat (endothermic) to return to the starting state.

Name: Equilibrium
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to understand that equilibrium is reached when the forward and reverse reactions occur at exactly the same rate in a closed system.
Description: Dynamic equilibrium occurs when the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal. In this state, the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant, provided the reaction occurs in a closed system where no substances can escape.

Name: The effect of changing conditions on equilibrium (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to make qualitative predictions about the effect of changes on systems at equilibrium using Le Chatelier’s Principle.
Description: Le Chatelier’s Principle states that if a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in conditions, the system will shift its equilibrium position to counteract that change. This allows scientists to predict how changes in temperature, pressure, or concentration will affect the yield.

Name: The effect of changing concentration (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to interpret given data to predict the effect of a change in concentration of a reactant or product on reactions at equilibrium.
Description: If the concentration of a reactant is increased, the system shifts to produce more products until equilibrium is restored. Conversely, if a product's concentration is decreased, the system shifts to use up more reactants to replace the lost product.

Name: The effect of temperature changes on equilibrium (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to interpret given data to predict the effect of a change in temperature on reactions at equilibrium.
Description: For a system at equilibrium, increasing the temperature favours the endothermic reaction (to absorb the extra heat). Decreasing the temperature favours the exothermic reaction (to release more heat). This directly impacts the relative amount of products at equilibrium.

Name: The effect of pressure changes on equilibrium (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to interpret given data to predict the effect of pressure changes on gaseous reactions at equilibrium.
Description: In gaseous systems, increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with the fewest molecules of gas (to reduce pressure). Decreasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with the most molecules of gas (to increase pressure).

Name: Crude oil, hydrocarbons and alkanes
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to recognise substances as alkanes given their formulae in various forms (C2H6 or displayed structural formulae).
Description: Crude oil is a finite resource formed over millions of years from the remains of ancient biomass, primarily plankton. It is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons, which are molecules containing only carbon and hydrogen. Most of these are alkanes, a homologous series of saturated hydrocarbons with the general formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$.

Name: Fractional distillation and petrochemicals
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to explain how fractional distillation works in terms of evaporation and condensation.
Description: Fractional distillation separates the various hydrocarbons in crude oil into 'fractions' based on their boiling points. These fractions serve as fuels (petrol, diesel, kerosene) and feedstock for the petrochemical industry to produce solvents, lubricants, and polymers.

Name: Properties of hydrocarbons
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recall how boiling point, viscosity, and flammability change with molecular size; write balanced equations for complete combustion.
Description: The physical properties of hydrocarbons—boiling point, viscosity, and flammability—are determined by molecular size. Shorter chains have lower boiling points and higher flammability. Complete combustion occurs when carbon and hydrogen are fully oxidised to produce carbon dioxide and water, releasing energy.

Name: Cracking and alkenes
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe general conditions for catalytic and steam cracking; recall the colour change for the bromine water test; balance chemical equations for cracking.
Description: Cracking involves breaking down long-chain hydrocarbons into smaller, more useful molecules using heat and a catalyst (catalytic cracking) or steam (steam cracking). The process produces alkanes for fuels and alkenes, which are unsaturated hydrocarbons used as starting materials for polymers.

Name: Structure and formulae of alkenes
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to recognise substances that are alkenes from their names or given formulae.
Description: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons that contain a $C=C$ double bond. They belong to a homologous series with the general formula $C_nH_{2n}$. Because they contain fewer hydrogen atoms than alkanes with the same number of carbons, they are more reactive.

Name: Reactions of alkenes
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe reactions and conditions for addition of hydrogen, water, and halogens; draw fully displayed structural formulae of first four alkenes and their addition products.
Description: Alkenes react with oxygen via combustion (often incomplete, producing smoky flames) and via addition reactions. In addition, atoms are added across the $C=C$ double bond, turning it into a single bond. Common addition reactions include hydrogenation, hydration (forming alcohols), and halogenation.

Name: Alcohols
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe reactions with sodium, air (combustion), water, and oxidising agents; recall main uses; know conditions for fermentation.
Description: Alcohols contain the functional group $-OH$. The first four members (methanol to butanol) are used as fuels and solvents. Ethanol is produced by the fermentation of sugars using yeast under warm, anaerobic conditions. Alcohols can be oxidised to form carboxylic acids.

Name: Carboxylic acids
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe reactions with carbonates, water, and alcohols; (HT only) explain why they are weak acids in terms of ionisation and pH.
Description: Carboxylic acids contain the functional group $-COOH$. They react with carbonates to produce a salt, water, and $CO_2$. In water, they only partially ionise, meaning they are classified as weak acids with a higher pH than strong acids of the same concentration.

Name: Addition polymerisation
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recognise addition polymers and monomers from diagrams; draw diagrams representing polymer formation; relate the repeating unit to the monomer.
Description: Addition polymerisation involves many small unsaturated molecules (monomers) joining together to form very large molecules (polymers). In this process, the repeating unit has exactly the same atoms as the monomer because no other molecules are produced as by-products.

Name: Condensation polymerisation (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to explain the basic principles of condensation polymerisation by reference to functional groups and repeating units.
Description: Condensation polymerisation involves monomers with two functional groups. When they react, they join together and usually lose a small molecule, such as water. Polyesters are a common example, formed from a diol and a dicarboxylic acid.

Name: Amino acids (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to understand that amino acids have two different functional groups and react by condensation polymerisation.
Description: Amino acids contain both an amine group ($-NH_2$) and a carboxylic acid group ($-COOH$). They polymerise through condensation to form polypeptides. Different amino acids combined in a chain produce diverse proteins essential for life.

Name: DNA and other naturally occurring polymers
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to name the types of monomers from which naturally occurring polymers are made.
Description: DNA is a large, double-helix molecule made from four different monomers called nucleotides. Other vital natural polymers include proteins (made of amino acids), and starch and cellulose (both made from glucose monomers).

Name: Pure substances
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to use melting point and boiling point data to distinguish pure from impure substances.
Description: In chemical terms, a pure substance is a single element or compound not mixed with any other substance. Pure substances have sharp, specific melting and boiling points, whereas mixtures melt or boil over a range of temperatures; this data is a key diagnostic tool for purity.

Name: Formulations
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify formulations given appropriate information.
Description: A formulation is a mixture designed as a useful product, created by mixing components in exact, carefully measured quantities. Each chemical in a formulation serves a specific purpose, such as in medicines, paints, fuels, alloys, and fertilizers.

Name: Chromatography
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain how paper chromatography separates mixtures; suggest methods to distinguish pure from impure substances; interpret chromatograms and determine Rf values to an appropriate number of significant figures.
Description: Chromatography separates mixtures based on how substances distribute themselves between a stationary phase and a mobile phase. The $R_f$ value (distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent) is unique to compounds in specific conditions. A pure substance produces a single spot, while a mixture separates into multiple spots.

Name: Test for hydrogen
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe the chemical test for hydrogen gas.
Description: The presence of hydrogen is confirmed using a burning splint held at the mouth of a test tube. If hydrogen is present, it burns rapidly with a characteristic "squeaky pop" sound.

Name: Test for oxygen
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe the chemical test for oxygen gas.
Description: The presence of oxygen is confirmed by inserting a glowing splint into a test tube containing the gas. Oxygen supports combustion, so it will cause the glowing splint to relight.

Name: Test for carbon dioxide
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe the chemical test for carbon dioxide gas.
Description: Carbon dioxide is identified using limewater (aqueous calcium hydroxide). When the gas is bubbled through or shaken with limewater, the solution turns milky or cloudy due to the formation of a white precipitate.

Name: Test for chlorine
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe the chemical test for chlorine gas.
Description: Chlorine is a reactive gas that acts as a bleaching agent. When damp litmus paper is exposed to chlorine gas, the paper is bleached and turns white.

Name: Flame tests
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify metal cations ($Li^+$, $Na^+$, $K^+$, $Ca^{2+}$, $Cu^{2+}$) based on distinctive flame colours.
Description: Flame tests are used to identify metal ions in a sample. Specific results include: Lithium (crimson), Sodium (yellow), Potassium (lilac), Calcium (orange-red), and Copper (green). Note that mixtures of ions can cause colours to be masked.

Name: Metal hydroxides
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: identify metal ions ($Al^{3+}$, $Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$, $Cu^{2+}$, $Fe^{2+}$, $Fe^{3+}$) using sodium hydroxide; write balanced equations for the production of insoluble hydroxides.
Description: Sodium hydroxide solution forms characteristic precipitates with specific metal ions. $Al^{3+}$, $Ca^{2+}$, and $Mg^{2+}$ form white precipitates (only $Al^{3+}$ redissolves in excess NaOH). Coloured precipitates include Copper(II) (blue), Iron(II) (green), and Iron(III) (brown).

Name: Carbonates
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify carbonate ions using chemical tests.
Description: Carbonates react with dilute acids to produce carbon dioxide gas. This gas is then bubbled through limewater to confirm the identity of the original carbonate through a milky/cloudy result.

Name: Halides
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify halide ions ($Cl^-$, $Br^-$, $I^-$) using silver nitrate.
Description: In the presence of dilute nitric acid, silver nitrate reacts with halide ions to form precipitates: Silver chloride (white), Silver bromide (cream), and Silver iodide (yellow).

Name: Sulfates
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify sulfate ions ($SO_4^{2-}$) using barium chloride.
Description: Sulfate ions in solution react with barium chloride in the presence of dilute hydrochloric acid to produce a white precipitate of barium sulfate.

Name: Instrumental methods
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to state the advantages of instrumental methods (accuracy, sensitivity, and speed) compared to chemical tests.
Description: Modern analysis relies on sophisticated instruments that provide high accuracy and sensitivity, allowing for the rapid detection of chemicals even in minute quantities. These are essential in fields like forensics and drug control.

Name: Flame emission spectroscopy
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to interpret instrumental results (line spectra) to identify metal ions and measure concentrations.
Description: This instrumental method involves passing light from a flame-heated sample through a spectroscope. The resulting line spectrum acts as a "fingerprint" to identify specific metal ions and calculate their concentration in the solution.

Name: The proportions of different gases in the atmosphere
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to identify the approximate proportions of nitrogen, oxygen, and other gases (carbon dioxide, water vapour, and noble gases) in the current atmosphere.
Description: For approximately 200 million years, the Earth's atmosphere has maintained a stable composition. It is predominantly Nitrogen (~80% or four-fifths) and Oxygen (~20% or one-fifth), with small percentages of various other gases including Carbon Dioxide, water vapour, and the noble gases.

Name: The Earth's early atmosphere
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: interpret evidence and evaluate different theories about the Earth’s early atmosphere given appropriate information.
Description: Earth's early atmosphere, dating back 4.6 billion years, was likely formed by intense volcanic activity releasing nitrogen, water vapour, and small amounts of methane and ammonia. It was primarily carbon dioxide with little to no oxygen, similar to modern-day Mars and Venus. Water vapour eventually condensed to form the oceans.

Name: How oxygen increased
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to recall the role of algae and plants in producing oxygen via photosynthesis and the timeline of animal evolution.
Description: Oxygen levels rose significantly about 2.7 billion years ago when algae first evolved and began photosynthesis ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$). Over the following billion years, the evolution of plants increased oxygen percentages to a level that supported the development of animal life.

Name: How carbon dioxide decreased
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe the main changes in the atmosphere over time and their likely causes; explain the formation of limestone, coal, crude oil, and natural gas.
Description: Atmospheric $CO_2$ was reduced through three main processes: absorption by algae and plants for photosynthesis, dissolution into oceans to form carbonate sediments/sedimentary rocks (like limestone), and the "locking up" of carbon within the remains of dead organisms that formed fossil fuels (coal, crude oil, and gas).

Name: Greenhouse gases
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe the greenhouse effect in terms of the interaction of short and long wavelength radiation with matter.
Description: Greenhouse gases, including water vapour, carbon dioxide, and methane, are vital for maintaining Earth's temperature to support life. They allow short-wavelength radiation from the sun to pass through but absorb the long-wavelength (thermal) radiation reflected from Earth's surface, trapping heat in the atmosphere.

Name: Human activities and greenhouse gases
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recall two human activities that increase $CO_2$ and methane; evaluate the quality of evidence in reports about climate change; recognize the importance of peer review.
Description: Human activities, specifically the burning of fossil fuels (releasing $CO_2$) and intensive cattle farming/landfill (releasing methane), are increasing greenhouse gas concentrations. While complex to model, peer-reviewed scientific consensus points to these activities as the primary cause of rising global surface temperatures.

Name: Global climate change
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe briefly four potential effects of global climate change; discuss the scale, risk, and environmental implications of these changes.
Description: Rising global temperatures lead to significant environmental risks. Potential effects include the melting of polar ice caps and rising sea levels, changes in rainfall patterns affecting food production, increased frequency/severity of storms, and shifts in the distribution and extinction of various species.

Name: The carbon footprint and its reduction
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe actions to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide and methane; give reasons why these actions may be limited.
Description: The carbon footprint measures the total greenhouse gas emissions over the full life cycle of a product, service, or event. Reduction strategies include using renewable energy, carbon capture, and dietary changes, though implementation is often limited by economic costs, lack of international agreement, or individual lifestyle choices.

Name: Atmospheric pollutants from fuels
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe how carbon monoxide, soot, sulfur dioxide, and oxides of nitrogen are produced; predict combustion products given fuel composition.
Description: Combustion of fuels containing carbon, hydrogen, and sulfur releases pollutants. Complete combustion produces $CO_2$; incomplete combustion produces toxic Carbon Monoxide and soot (carbon particulates). High-temperature engine reactions produce Oxides of Nitrogen, while sulfur impurities in fuel produce Sulfur Dioxide.

Name: Properties and effects of atmospheric pollutants
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to describe and explain the specific health and environmental problems caused by carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and particulates.
Description: Pollutants have severe impacts: Carbon Monoxide is a "silent killer" (toxic, colourless, odourless); Sulfur Dioxide and Oxides of Nitrogen cause respiratory issues and acid rain; Particulates lead to global dimming (reducing sunlight reaching Earth) and cause various human health problems.

Name: Using the Earth's resources and sustainable development
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: state examples of natural products that are supplemented or replaced by agricultural and synthetic products; distinguish between finite and renewable resources; extract and interpret information about resources from charts, graphs and tables.
Description: Humans rely on Earth's natural resources for warmth, shelter, food, and transport. Sustainable development is a central concept, defined as meeting current needs without compromising future generations. Chemistry improves industrial processes to provide new products while balancing the use of renewable resources (which can be replaced) and finite resources (which are limited and processed for energy and materials).

Name: Potable water
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: distinguish between potable water and pure water; describe the differences in treatment of ground water and salty water; give reasons for the steps used to produce potable water.
Description: Potable water is water safe for human consumption, containing low levels of dissolved salts and microbes. In the UK, fresh water is treated via filtration and sterilisation (using chlorine, ozone, or UV). In areas with limited fresh water, desalination is used through energy-intensive processes like distillation or reverse osmosis.

Name: Waste water treatment
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to comment on the relative ease of obtaining potable water from waste, ground, and salt water.
Description: Waste water from urban, agricultural, and industrial sources requires multi-stage treatment to remove organic matter and harmful microbes. This includes screening/grit removal, sedimentation to produce sludge and effluent, anaerobic digestion of sludge, and aerobic biological treatment of effluent.

Name: Alternative methods of extracting metals (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to evaluate alternative biological methods of metal extraction, given appropriate information.
Description: As high-grade ores become scarce, biological methods are used for low-grade ores. Phytomining uses plants to absorb metal compounds, which are then harvested and burned to ash. Bioleaching uses bacteria to produce leachate solutions. Metals are then extracted from these solutions via displacement (using scrap iron) or electrolysis.

Name: Life cycle assessment
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: carry out simple comparative LCAs for products like shopping bags; interpret LCAs of materials; identify that LCAs involve value judgements and are not purely objective.
Description: Life cycle assessments (LCAs) evaluate the environmental impact of a product through four stages: raw material extraction, manufacturing/packaging, use during lifetime, and disposal/transport. While energy and waste can be quantified, pollutant effects often require subjective value judgements.

Name: Ways of reducing the use of resources
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to evaluate ways of reducing the use of limited resources, given appropriate information.
Description: Sustainability is improved by reducing the use of materials, reusing products (like glass bottles), and recycling. Recycling metals involves melting and recasting, which conserves energy and reduces the environmental impact of mining and quarrying.

Name: Corrosion and its prevention
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: describe experiments showing that air and water are necessary for rusting; explain sacrificial protection in terms of relative reactivity.
Description: Corrosion is the chemical destruction of materials by the environment. Rusting (specifically of iron) requires both oxygen and water. Prevention methods include barrier coatings (paint, grease, electroplating), oxide layers (aluminium), or sacrificial protection where a more reactive metal like zinc is used to galvanise the iron.

Name: Alloys as useful materials
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recall a use of specific alloys (bronze, brass, gold, steel, aluminium); interpret and evaluate the composition of alloys.
Description: Alloys are mixtures of metals designed for specific properties. Bronze (Cu+Sn) and Brass (Cu+Zn) have decorative/mechanical uses. Gold is alloyed for hardness. Steels are iron alloys with carbon; high carbon is strong/brittle, low carbon is soft, and stainless steel (Cr+Ni) resists corrosion.

Name: Ceramics, polymers and composites
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: explain how LD and HD poly(ethene) are produced; explain the difference between thermosoftening and thermosetting polymers; compare physical properties of materials; select appropriate materials for uses.
Description: Glass is made from sand and carbonates, while ceramics are made from shaped clay fired in a furnace. Polymers vary based on monomers and conditions (LD vs HD polyethene). Thermosoftening polymers melt when heated; thermosetting polymers do not due to cross-links. Composites consist of a reinforcement material surrounded by a matrix binder.

Name: The Haber process
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recall raw material sources for nitrogen and hydrogen; (HT only) explain the trade-off between rate and equilibrium position in commercial conditions ($450^\circ\text{C}$, 200 atm, iron catalyst).
Description: The Haber process produces ammonia ($NH_3$) for fertilisers. Nitrogen is obtained from the air and hydrogen from natural gas. The reversible reaction uses an iron catalyst. High pressure favours the forward reaction, while a compromised temperature is used to balance high rate against a lower equilibrium yield.

Name: Production and uses of NPK fertilisers
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: recall the names of salts produced when phosphate rock is treated with acids; compare industrial production with laboratory preparations.
Description: NPK fertilisers are formulations of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium to improve crop yield. Ammonia is used to make ammonium salts and nitric acid. Phosphate rock is mined and treated with nitric, sulfuric, or phosphoric acid to produce soluble salts suitable for use as fertiliser.

Name: Understand how scientific methods and theories develop over time
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Give examples to show how scientific methods and theories have changed over time; Explain why new data led to changes in models or theories; Decide if data supports a theory.
Description: Focuses on the historical development of science. Recognizes that new experimental evidence leads to models being changed or replaced, using examples like the evolution of the atomic model.

Name: Use a variety of models to solve problems and develop scientific explanations
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use representational, spatial, descriptive, and mathematical models; Recognize/draw/interpret diagrams; Translate data to a model; Make predictions or calculate quantities.
Description: Involves using physical or conceptual models (like dot-and-cross diagrams or the particle model) to explain chemical phenomena and make predictions about unfamiliar facts.

Name: Appreciate the power and limitations of science and consider ethical issues
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Explain why data is needed to answer questions; Understand why data may be uncertain or unavailable; Outline simple ethical arguments regarding new technology.
Description: Addresses the boundaries of scientific knowledge and the moral implications of technological advancements, such as the use of nanoparticles or nuclear energy.

Name: Explain everyday and technological applications of science
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Evaluate personal, social, economic, and environmental implications; Make decisions based on the evaluation of evidence and arguments.
Description: Focuses on how chemistry is applied in the real world (e.g., the Haber Process) and evaluating the balance between industrial needs and environmental impact.

Name: Evaluate risks in practical science and wider societal context
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Identify hazards in technologies; Contrast perceived risk vs. measured risk; Suggest methods to reduce harm.
Description: Requires understanding the difference between a hazard (something that can cause harm) and risk (the chance of that harm occurring), applied to lab safety and global issues.

Name: Recognise the importance of peer review and communicating results
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Explain how peer review detects false claims; Distinguish between peer-reviewed reports and popular media.
Description: Covers the validation process of scientific data. Ensures that claims are checked by other scientists to establish a consensus and prevent bias.

Name: Use scientific theories and explanations to develop hypotheses
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Suggest a hypothesis to explain given observations or data.
Description: The ability to propose a scientific starting point for an investigation based on existing knowledge or early observations.

Name: Plan experiments or devise procedures
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Describe practical procedures; Explain design choices; Identify independent, dependent, and control variables.
Description: Covers experimental design. Requires students to understand how to keep an investigation 'fair' by manipulating one variable and controlling others.

Name: Select appropriate techniques and apparatus
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Choose the correct material, instrument, or technique for a specific purpose and justify the choice.
Description: Involves selecting lab equipment (e.g., using a pipette instead of a measuring cylinder for higher accuracy) based on the precision required.

Name: Carry out experiments with regard for accuracy and safety
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Manipulate apparatus correctly; Identify main hazards; Suggest methods to reduce risk.
Description: Focuses on the practical execution of experiments, including safe handling of chemicals (e.g., using a fume cupboard) and accurate measurement techniques.

Name: Recognise when to apply sampling techniques
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Ensure samples collected are representative; Suggest and describe appropriate sampling techniques.
Description: Applied when testing large environments, such as testing the pH of soil in a field or pollutants in a river, to ensure the data represents the whole.

Name: Make and record observations and measurements
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use a range of apparatus; Read scales and record data appropriately with units.
Description: Ensures that observations (qualitative) and measurements (quantitative) are logged systematically during required practicals.

Name: Evaluate methods and suggest possible improvements
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Assess if measurements were precise; Evaluate method validity; Suggest further investigations.
Description: Refers to the 'post-lab' phase where the experimental design is critiqued for errors and ways to increase reliability are proposed.

Name: Presenting observations and other data using appropriate methods
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Construct and interpret frequency tables, bar charts, and histograms; Plot two variables from data.
Description: Focuses on the visual representation of results using standard scientific conventions.

Name: Translating data from one form to another
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Translate data between graphical and numeric form.
Description: The skill of moving between raw data tables and their corresponding visual representations.

Name: Carrying out and representing mathematical analysis
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use significant figures; Find mean and range; Change the subject of an equation; Determine gradients and intercepts.
Description: The mathematical toolkit for chemistry, including calculating the slope of a tangent to determine the rate of reaction.

Name: Representing distributions of results and estimations of uncertainty
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Apply the idea that measurements have uncertainty; Use the range about the mean as a measure of uncertainty.
Description: Recognizes that no measurement is perfect. Uses the spread of data to calculate the plus/minus margin of error.

Name: Interpreting observations and other data
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use data to make predictions; Recognize patterns and trends; Draw conclusions from observations.
Description: Covers the analysis of results to state exactly what the data shows, such as identifying proportional relationships.

Name: Presenting reasoned explanations
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Comment on whether data is consistent with a hypothesis; Identify the best explanation for data.
Description: Linking the results back to the original theory to see if the hypothesis was supported or refuted.

Name: Being objective and identifying potential sources of error
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Evaluate data in terms of accuracy, precision, repeatability, and reproducibility; Identify random and systematic errors.
Description: Distinguishes between repeatability (you get same result) and reproducibility (others get same result). Identifies and explains anomalous values.

Name: Communicating the scientific rationale
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Present coherent and logically structured responses; Report findings using verbal and symbolic forms.
Description: The final stage of an investigation where findings are communicated clearly, often required in extended 6-mark practical responses.

Name: Use scientific vocabulary and definitions
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use technical terms correctly in context.
Description: Ensures that the correct chemical language (e.g., 'aqueous', 'precipitate', 'molar') is used to prevent ambiguity.

Name: Recognise the importance of scientific quantities
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Understand how different quantities are determined.
Description: Understanding the physical meaning behind measurements like mass, volume, and temperature.

Name: Use SI units and IUPAC chemical nomenclature
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use standard units (kg, g, m, J) and follow international naming conventions for chemicals.
Description: Consistency in naming and measurement, such as using 'Copper(II) sulfate' instead of just 'blue copper crystals'.

Name: Use prefixes and powers of ten for orders of magnitude
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Use prefixes like kilo, centi, milli, micro, and nano.
Description: Essential for calculations involving different scales, especially in nanoscience and atomic structure.

Name: Interconvert units
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Convert between units (e.g. cm3 to dm3 or g to kg).
Description: A critical skill for quantitative chemistry calculations like concentration and molarity.

Name: Use an appropriate number of significant figures
Learning Outcomes: Student should be able to: Round answers based on the least certain measurement in a calculation.
Description: Ensures that the precision of a final answer reflects the precision of the data collected during the experiment.

Name: Preparation of a pure, dry sample of a soluble salt from an insoluble oxide or carbonate, using a Bunsen burner to heat dilute acid and a water bath or electric heater to evaporate the solution.
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: safe use of appropriate heating devices and techniques including use of a Bunsen burner and a water bath or electric heater, safe use of appropriate heating devices and techniques including use of a Bunsen burner and a water bath or electric heater, use of appropriate apparatus and techniques for conducting chemical reactions, including appropriate reagents, safe use of a range of equipment to purify and/or separate chemical mixtures including evaporation, filtration, crystallisation, afe use and careful handling of liquids and solids, including careful mixing of reagents under controlled conditions
Description: Prepare a soluble salt by reacting an insoluble base with an acid, adding excess solid to ensure all acid reacts. Filter to remove unreacted solid, then evaporate the solution and allow crystals to form by crystallisation before drying the salt.

Name: Determination of the reacting volumes of solutions of a strong acid and a strong alkali by titration. Determination of the concentration of one of the solutions in mol/dm3 and g/dm3 from the reacting volumes and the known concentration of the other solution.
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use of appropriate apparatus to make and record a range of measurements accurately, including volume of liquids, the determination of concentrations of strong acids and strong alkalis.
Description: Determine the concentration of an acid or alkali by carrying out a titration using a pipette, burette, and indicator. Add one solution to another until the end-point colour change is reached, then use volume measurements to calculate concentration, repeating for accuracy.

Name: Investigate what happens when aqueous solutions are electrolysed using inert electrodes. This should be an investigation involving developing a hypothesis.
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use of appropriate apparatus and techniques for conducting and monitoring chemical reactions, use of appropriate apparatus and techniques to draw, set up and use electrochemical cells for separation and production of elements and compounds, use of appropriate qualitative reagents and techniques to analyse and identify unknown samples or products including gas tests for hydrogen, oxygen and chlorine.
Description: Investigate electrolysis by passing an electric current through an electrolyte using inert electrodes, then identify the products formed at each electrode. Use observations and gas tests to determine the substances produced and relate them to the ions present in the solution.

Name: Investigate the variables that affect temperature changes in reacting solutions such as, eg acid plus metals, acid plus carbonates, neutralisations, displacement of metals.
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use of appropriate apparatus to make and record a range of measurements accurately, including mass, temperature, and volume of liquids, use of appropriate apparatus and techniques for conducting and monitoring chemical reactions, making and recording of appropriate observations during chemical reactions including changes in temperature, safe use and careful handling of gases, liquids and solids, including careful mixing of reagents under controlled conditions, using appropriate apparatus to explore chemical changes,
Description: Measure temperature changes during chemical reactions using a simple calorimeter (polystyrene cup). Record initial and final temperatures to determine the energy change, identifying reactions as exothermic or endothermic while minimising heat loss to improve accuracy.

Name: Investigate how changes in concentration affect the rates of reactions by a method involving measuring the volume of a gas produced and a method involving a change in colour or turbidity. This should be an investigation involving developing a hypothesis.
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: use of appropriate apparatus to make and record a range of measurements accurately, including mass, time, temperature, and volume of liquids and gases, use of appropriate apparatus and techniques for conducting and monitoring chemical reactions, making and recording of appropriate observations during chemical reactions including the measurement of rates of reaction by a variety of methods such as production of gas and colour change, safe use and careful handling of gases, liquids and solids, including careful mixing of reagents under controlled conditions, using appropriate apparatus to explore chemical changes.
Description: Investigate the rate of a chemical reaction by measuring gas production or observing a change such as turbidity (e.g. disappearing cross). Vary a factor like concentration, record the time taken or volume of gas produced, and use this data to determine how the factor affects reaction rate while controlling variables.

Name: Investigate how paper chromatography can be used to separate and tell the difference between coloured substances. Students should calculate Rf values.
Learning Outcomes:
Students should be able to: use of appropriate apparatus to make and record a range of measurements accurately, safe use of a range of equipment to purify and/or separate chemical mixtures including
chromatography.
Description: Separate mixtures of substances (e.g. dyes) using paper chromatography by allowing a solvent to carry components up the paper. Measure distances travelled by the solvent and substances to calculate Rf values and identify compounds.

Name: Use of chemical tests to identify the ions in unknown single ionic compounds covering the ions
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: safe use of a Bunsen burner, use of appropriate qualitative reagents and techniques to analyse and identify unknown samples or products including gas tests, flame tests, precipitation reactions.
Description: Identify different gases and ions using chemical tests. Carry out gas tests (e.g. hydrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide, chlorine) and test for anions and cations using reagents that produce characteristic colour changes or precipitates, including flame tests and reactions with acids or sodium hydroxide.

Name: Analysis and purification of water samples from different sources, including pH, dissolved solids and distillation
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: safe use of appropriate heating devices and techniques including use of a Bunsen burner and a water bath or electric heater, use of appropriate apparatus and techniques for the measurement of pH in different situations, safe use of a range of equipment to purify and/or separate chemical mixtures including evaporation, distillation.
Description: Analyse water samples by measuring pH and determining dissolved solids through evaporation, then purify water using simple distillation. Assess purity by measuring boiling point and comparing to that of pure water.
